flashcards.front: What is exception chaining in Java?
flashcards.back: Exception chaining is a mechanism that allows associating one exception with another. When an exception occurs, a method can catch it and throw a new exception while preserving information about the original exception as the cause.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of initCause() method?
flashcards.back: initCause() is used to associate the original exception (cause) with a newly thrown exception. It sets the cause of the calling exception to the specified Throwable object.

flashcards.front: How do you retrieve the original exception in a chain?
flashcards.back: Use getCause() method on the exception object. It returns the Throwable that was set as the cause of the current exception, or null if no cause was set.

flashcards.front: Which class supports exception chaining in Java?
flashcards.back: The Throwable class and all its subclasses (Error, Exception, RuntimeException) support exception chaining through initCause() and getCause() methods.

flashcards.front: Why is exception chaining important in layered applications?
flashcards.back: In multi-layered apps (like MVC), errors occur deep in lower layers but need to be communicated to upper layers. Chaining preserves the root cause while allowing each layer to add its contextual information.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between chained and nested exceptions?
flashcards.back: Chained exceptions use initCause()/getCause() to link exceptions linearly (cause-effect chain). Nested exceptions involve a try-catch block inside another try-catch (structural nesting).

flashcards.front: Name the constructors that support exception chaining.
flashcards.back: Throwable(Throwable cause) and Throwable(String message, Throwable cause) are constructors that directly accept a cause. Other classes like Exception and RuntimeException also have these constructors.

flashcards.front: How does chained exception help in debugging?
flashcards.back: Chained exceptions preserve the complete stack trace and error information from all layers, making it easier to trace back to the root cause rather than seeing only the outermost exception.

flashcards.front: When should you use exception chaining?
flashcards.back: Use chaining when: 1) Catching an exception from a lower layer, 2) Throwing a new exception with more context, 3) Building multi-tier applications, 4) Hiding implementation-specific exceptions from API users.

flashcards.front: Can you chain multiple exceptions together?
flashcards.back: Yes, you can create a chain of exceptions. Each exception can have its own cause, forming a linked list-like structure that can be traversed using getCause() repeatedly.

flashcards.front: What is the root cause in exception chaining?
flashcards.back: The root cause is the original exception at the end of the chain that has no further cause (getCause() returns null). This is typically the actual error that initiated the chain.

flashcards.front: How does printStackTrace() work with chained exceptions?
flashcards.back: printStackTrace() prints the stack trace of the exception and all its chained causes, showing the full chain of exceptions from outermost to root cause.

flashcards.front: What is the alternative to initCause()?
flashcards.back: You can use constructors that accept a cause parameter directly, such as new Exception(String message, Throwable cause), instead of calling initCause() separately.

flashcards.front: Why avoid exposing low-level exceptions to high-level API users?
flashcards.back: Low-level exceptions (like SQLException, IOException) expose implementation details. Chaining allows throwing high-level meaningful exceptions (like BusinessException) while preserving the original cause.
